stem_zh: Q1-A Suggested Solution-Helliwell Co (a)Investment in Carter In accordance with IFRS 3 Business Combinations,consideration transferred to achieve control over a subsidiary must be measured at fair value.The contingent consideration had a fair value of $3.56 million at the acquisition date and should be recognised at this amount.A corresponding entry should be made to liabilities. The subsequent increase in the fair value of the consideration is a result of Carter's performance,which is a post-acquisition event.This increase of $0.94 million($4.5m -$3.56m)willbe added to the carrying amount of the liability,and it willalso be recognised as an expense in the consolidated statement of profit or loss in the year ended 31 December 20X7. IFRS 3 states that the identifiable net assets of a subsidiary should be measured at fair value at the acquisition date.The film rights have not been recognised in Carter's separate financial statements,but they must be recognised as an intangible asset in the consolidated financial statements at acquisition.This is because the rights meet the definition of an asset and because they are identifiable. The film rights should be measured at their fair value;however,this was only determined provisionally at the acquisition date.IFRS 3 permits the fair value of an item at acquisition to be confirmed during a measurement period of up to one year after the acquisition date.When calculating goodwill (or a gain on bargain purchase), the film rights should be measured at the confirmed fair value of $1.6 million,rather than the provisional value of $1.4 million. At the acquisition date,Carter has a contingent liability as a result of the legal case against it.It should be recognised at its fair value of $430,000 in the consolidated financial statements as at the acquisition date. A gain on bargain purchase arises.This is calculated as: Cash consideration Contingent consideration Non-controlling interest Fair value of identifiable net assets acquired Share capital Retained earnings Rights to film Contingent liability $'000 8,900 26,600 1,600 (430) $'000 24,000 3,560 27,560 7,120 Bargain purchase (36,670) (1,990) The $1.99 million bargain purchase should be recognised as income in the consolidated statement of profit or loss for the year ended 31 December 20X7. However,before this,IFRS 3 states that Helliwell should reassess whether it has correctly identified all the assets acquired and liabilities assumed,as well as reviewing the procedures used to measure these. Even though the NCl in Carter is measured at fair value,the full amount of the bargain purchase should be allocated to the owners of Helliwell and accumulated in consolidated retained earnings. (b)Investment in Bhalla Helliwell has joint control over Bhalla.This means that Bhalla is a joint venture of Helliwell,and that Helliwell should apply equity accounting to its investment. The investment in Bhalla has been initially measured at cost in the consolidated financial statements,which is the correct treatment.Subsequently,Helliwellshould recognise 35%of Bhalla's profits in the consolidated statement of profit or loss,as wellas recognising a corresponding increase in the carrying amount of the investment. Bhalla has a different functional currency to Helliwell's presentation currency and so the principles of IAS 21 The Effects of Changes in Foreign Exchange Rates should be applied.Therefore,an exchange rate of $1:Rm22 is applied to the initial cost of the investment,an exchange rate of $1:Rm20 is applied to Helliwell's share of Bhalla's profits,and an exchange rate of $1:Rm19 is applied to the carrying amount of the investment at the year end. As the exchange rate has moved during this period,an exchange difference arises. This is calculated as follows: Rm'000 Exchange rate $'000 Cost 136,400 $1:Rm22 6,200 Share of profits Rm48m×3/12m x 35% 4,200 $1:Rm20 210 Exchange difference (bal. fig.) 990 Carrying amount at 31 December 20X7 140,600 $1:Rm19 7,400 The exchange gain of $990,000 should be recognised as other comprehensive income in the consolidated financial statements.It should be presented as an item that will be reclassified to profit or loss when specific conditions are met.Exchange gains and losses on the translation of foreign operations are accumulated in a separate equity reserve. (c)Extract from consolidated statement of profit or loss and other comprehensive income. Q2-A Suggested Solution-Gillam Co (a)Sale and leaseback The transfer of the property on 31 December 20X7 meets the criteria in IFRS 15 Revenue from Contracts with Customers to be classified as a sale. This means that, in accordance with IFRS 16 Leases,Gillam should derecognise the investment property,and recognise a lease liability and right-of-use asset.It should also recognise the $22 million cash proceeds as well as a proportion of any gain or loss on transfer of the property. Since the proceeds exceed the fair value of the property,the excess of $2 million is treated as additional financing provided by the buyer/lessor.Gillam must recognise this amount as a financial liability. A lease liability is initially measured at the present value of future lease payments, including all fixed and fixed-in-substance lease payments.Variable lease payments are excluded from the liability unless the variability relates to a rate or index. In the case of a green lease in which payments are reduced if emissions targets are met,the minimum amount that must be paid is a fixed lease payment and any amount that may be paid in excess of this is a variable lease payment.Therefore, regardless of the likelihood of meeting emissions targets,Gillam's lease liability should be measured based on annual lease payments of $1.25 million.This means that Gillam should recognise a lease liability at 31 December 20X7 of $9.3 million. The right-of-use asset is measured at the proportion of the previous carrying amount of the investment property that relates to the 10-year right of use.This is $10.55 million($9.3m +$1.25m)/$20mx$20m). In this instance,no gain or loss arises on the transfer of the property.This is because the $20 million carrying amount of the property is equal to its fair value. On 31 December 20X7,a lease payment of $1.4 million was made.Of this amount, $1.25 million relates to an immediate payment of the lease liability and will therefore reduce its carrying amount.The remaining $150,000 is a variable lease payment and should be recognised in profit or loss. The sale and leaseback is recognised on 31 December as follows: Dr Bank($22m-$1.4m) $20.6m Dr Right-of-use asset $10.55m Dr Profit or loss $0.15m Cr Lease liability $9.3m Cr Financial liability $2m Cr Investment property $20m The right-of-use asset is an investment property and Gillam applies the fair value model to its investment properties.This means that,at 31 December 20X7,the right- of-use asset must be measured at fair value.Gillam should apply IFRS 13 Fair Value Measurement to determine the fair value.Any difference between the fair value and the $10.55 million carrying amount willbe recognised in the statement of profit or loss. (b)Professional and ethical issues In the email to Shammi,Bimal suggests that the emissions targets set by the lessor will be met in future years;however,his only basis for this assertion is that Westside was built in accordance with the Sustainable Building Guidelines.Bimal's instruction to Shammi appears to be driven by a desire to meet bank covenants,regardless of whether the emissions targets are actually met,which suggests a lack of both integrity and objectivity on his part,as well as poor professional judgement. Bimal also suggests that the leaseback liability should be recognised based on lease payments of $1.25 million per year because the emissions targets are highly likely to be met.This assertion indicates that Bimal may not have a clear understanding of the requirements of IFRS 16.He is correct in terms of the measurement of the liability,but his rationale for this decision is incorrect.Even if emissions targets are not expected to be met,the lease liability should be recognised based on payments of $1.25 million per year because these are the fixed element of future lease payments.This puts Bimal's professional competence in question and suggests that he may not have maintained his technical knowledge during his period of leave. Bimal has instructed Shammi to focus on Gillam's sustainable building practices and resulting lower lease payments when drafting the company's sustainability disclosures.He has also said that there is not a lot else to write about.This amounts to greenwashing,in other words,misleading users of sustainability disclosures by leading them to believe that Gillam is doing more to protect the environment and society than it is.Focusing on sustainable building practices used in one construction project,and the resulting financial incentives,masks the fact that Gillam operates in construction,which consumes significant levels of raw materials and natural resources and generates high levels of carbon emissions.Furthermore,the company no longer adheres to sustainable building guidelines due to the costs of implementation.This type of greenwashing is unethical and contravenes the ethical principle of integrity. Bimal's instruction to Shammi also indicates that he is unaware of the requirements of IFRS S1 General Requirements for Disclosure of Sustainability-related Financial Information,which includes faithful representation as a conceptual foundation.In other words,sustainability disclosures should provide a complete,neutral and accurate description of sustainability-related risks and opportunities.They should,for Gillam,reflect the risks associated with its normal business practices and the industry within which it operates. Given that an overriding ethical issue is Bimal's apparent lack of understanding of more recent IFRS Accounting Standards and IFRS Sustainability Disclosure Standards,Shammi should,in the first instance raise this with him and suggest that he bridge any knowledge gap through focused training. Shammi should also raise her concerns about Bimal's apparent lack of integrity with him and explain the concept of greenwashing to him.She should retain a record of all conversations with Bimal. If Bimal does not accept Shammi's concerns,Shammi may approach other Board members or the audit committee,or may contact the ACCA helpline to seek advice as to how to proceed. Detailed Marking Guide-Gillam Co (a) Discussion and application of the following to the scenario: Variable payments Liability and asset measurement Gain/loss in P/L Investment properties (b) Discussion and application of the following to the scenario: Ethical principles Actions Professional skills marks Total Marks 3 5 1 乙 I 乙 I 8 max 8 4 10 max 2 20

Q3-A Suggested Solution-Eddison Co (a) (i)Share options The grant of the share options is an equity-settled share-based payment to employees.IFRS 2 Share-based Payment requires that this is recognised as an expense over the vesting period,with a corresponding credit posted to a separate component of equity. The transaction is measured by reference to the fair value of the share options at the grant date. The number of options that willvest depends on a service condition,being a requirement that managers remain employed by Eddison until 31 December 20X8. At each reporting date,the measurement of the transaction is updated to reflect the expected number of options that will vest. In the year ended 31 December 20X7,the expense recognised in profit or loss is calculated as: $'000 Cumulative expense: $2.80×(200×10,000×90%)×2/3 3,360 Expense recognised in 20X6: (1,792) 1,568 The equity balance at 31 December 20X7 is $3.36 million. The carrying amount of the transaction in the statement of financial position is zero; however,the transaction does have a tax base,which is equal to the intrinsic value of the share options.The intrinsic value is calculated as the difference between the exercise price and current market price.At 31 December 20X7,this is $3($11.50- $8.50)per option. The total tax base of the transaction is therefore $3.6 million ($3×(200×10,000x 90%)×2/3).This is also the deductible temporary difference. The deferred tax asset in relation to the share options at 31 December 20X7 is $900,000($3.6m x25%),which is an increase of $796,000($900,000-$104,000). The amount of this increase that is recognised in profit or loss is restricted so that the cumulative credit to profit or loss does not exceed tax relating to the cumulative share option expense.Any excess amount is credited to equity. $'000 in 20X7Cumulative share option expense 3,360 Tax thereon(25%×3,360,000) 840 Deferred tax asset recognised in profit or loss in 20X6 (104) Deferred tax asset that may be recognised in profit or loss 736 in 20X7 Therefore,$60,000 of the increase to the deferred tax asset ($796,000-$736,000)is credited to equity. (a)(ii)Deferred tax and the Conceptual Framework Eddison must recognise a deferred tax asset in relation to the share option scheme. The Conceptual Framework defines an asset as a present economic resource controlled by an entity as a result of past events.An economic resource is a right that has the potential to produce benefits. There is little doubt that the share option scheme provides Eddison with potential benefits,since Eddison's taxable profits will be reduced when the share options are exercised,hence resulting in lower tax payable. Tax relief is,however,dependent on Eddison managers exercising their options;if none of the managers were to exercise their options,because for example the market price of a share at the exercise date fell below the exercise price,Eddison would not benefit from any tax relief. It is therefore questionable whether Eddison has a right to potential economic benefits at 31 December 20X7,since access to these benefits is contingent on future events outside its control.It is also questionable whether Eddison has control of the potential economic resource as a result of a past event because at the year-end the options have not been exercised and it does not have the present ability to direct the use of the potential tax relief or obtain economic benefits from it. (b)(i)Investment in associate The investment in Lupton is classified as an associate.It should be accounted for using the equity method in the Eddison Group's consolidated financial statements. The investment is initially measured at its cost of $13.9 million,and subsequently increases by Eddison's share of Lupton's post-acquisition profits. Since the cost of the investment in Lupton on 1 September 20X7 was less than the share of Lupton's identifiable net assets at that date,Eddison's share of Lupton's post acquisition profits includes an adjustment to recognise the difference as income. Share of profits (40%x$5.1mx4/12) Share of identifiable net assets acquired (40% X $38m) Cost of investment Required adjustment Share of profits of Lupton $'000 15,200 (13,900) $'000 680 The carrying amount of the investment in Lupton at 31 December 20X7 in the consolidated statement of financial position is therefore $15.88 million($13.9m + $1.98m).The share of profits presented in the consolidated statement of profit or loss is $1.98 million. The tax base of Lupton is its cost of $13.9 million and therefore a taxable temporary difference of $1.98 million($15.88m-$13.9m)arises.This temporary difference will reverse when Lupton's profits are paid as dividends;however,Eddison does not control Lupton's dividends and so cannot control the timing of that reversal. Therefore,Eddison should recognise a deferred tax liability of $495,000 (25%x $1.98m)at 31 December 20X7,as well as a corresponding tax charge in the statement of profit or loss. (b)(ii)Deferred tax and the Conceptual Framework Eddison must recognise a deferred tax liability in relation to the investment in Lupton. A liability is defined in the Conceptual Framework as a present obligation to transfer an economic resource as a result of past events.An obligation is a duty or responsibility that an entity has no practical ability to avoid. Eddison will be required to pay tax in the future if the taxable temporary difference is reversed through the payment of dividends by Lupton to Eddison.For Eddison to pay tax equal to the full deferred tax liability recognised,Lupton would have to distribute all of its profits made since 1 September 20X7.There is no indication that this will happen. Therefore,at 31 December 20X7,Eddison does not have an obligation as there has been no past event.No dividends have been received from Lupton and therefore Eddison is not required to pay any tax. Q4-A Suggested Solution-Fuller Co (a)(i)Gopizza In respect of the contract with Gopizza,Fuller's promises were to add Gopizza's menu to its online platform and then to take customer orders,pass them on to Gopizza and deliver them for a period of 12 months.Fuller receives consideration only if customer orders are placed and no accounting entries are required until this time. As a result of each customer contract,Fuller promises to arrange a food order with Gopizza.If a customer chooses the delivery option,then Fuller also promises to deliver the food.IFRS 15 Revenue from Contracts with Customers states that promises in a contract form separate performance obligations if they are capable of being distinct and they are distinct in the context of the contract. The two promises made by Fuller are capable of being distinct since customers could use the platform to order Gopizza food and then collect it themselves from the restaurant.Therefore,arranging a food order and delivering a food order form two separate performance obligations for Fuller. In respect of the performance obligation to arrange food orders,Fuller is acting as an agent of Gopizza.Fuller is not a principal because it does not control the food order before it is transferred to the customer.This is evidenced by the following facts: · Fuller does not have primary responsibility for the customer being satisfied with the food order; · Fuller does not bear inventory risk that ingredients will be wasted if not used; and · Fuller does not set the selling price of the items on the restaurant's online menu. Since Fuller is an agent in the performance obligation to arrange food orders,it should only recognise revenue equal to the amount of commission that it receives from Gopizza.This is equal to 5%of the selling price of the food.This amounts to $4,950 (5%x$99,000)in the year ended 31 December 20X7. In respect of the performance obligation to deliver food orders,Fuller is acting as principal.It should therefore recognise the gross amount charged to customers of $21,000 as its revenue. The total revenue that Fuller should therefore recognise in the year ended 31 December 20X7 in relation to Gopizza orders placed on fullerfood.com is $25,950 ($4,950+$21,000). (a)(ii)Vouchers IFRS 15 is applied only when a contract is approved by the parties to it.The issue of the vouchers in May 20X8 would not create a binding contract between Fuller and its customers,because customers have no obligation to use the voucher. In the absence of a contract,no accounting entries are made.Fuller should not recognise a liability for the costs associated with redemption of the vouchers in May 20X8. The vouchers are only accounted for when they are used,because at this stage a such as those proposed by Fuller as consideration payable to a customer.contract between Fuller and its customer is formed.IFRS 15 describes vouchers such as those proposed by Fuller as consideration payable to a customer. Unless consideration payable to a customer is payment for a distinct good or service, it should be accounted for as a reduction in revenue.The vouchers issued by Fuller would not form consideration for a distinct good or service received. Therefore,when a customer redeems a free delivery voucher,Fuller should only recognise revenue equal to 5%of the listed price of the items ordered. (b)Technology Accounting treatment The data management software and Al technology are both purchased intangible assets.In accordance with IAS 38 Intangible Assets,these should be recognised at their total cost of $2.02 million($820,000+$1,200,000).These intangible assets will be subsequently amortised over their estimated useful lives. The original algorithms that were included in the purchased Al technology are refined and improved on an ongoing basis by Fuller employees.IAS 38 states that subsequent expenditure should be recognised as part of the cost of the asset only if it meets the definition of an intangible asset and the IAS 38 recognition criteria. Amounts spent to refine and improve existing algorithms within the Al technology appear to meet the definition and recognition criteria because: · the improved Al technology is identifiable as a result of being separable (it may be sold,together with the data management software); · it is non-monetary and has no physical substance; · the technology is controlled by Fuller because it can obtain the economic benefits resulting from it.Security measures ensure that control is maintained; · economic benefits are expected to flow from the use of the improved algorithms within the Al technology in the form of higher revenues and reduced costs;and · the staff costs incurred to improve the algorithms can be measured reliably. The $630,000 staff costs to improve the algorithms within the Al technology should be included within the carrying amount of intangible assets and amortised over their useful lives. However,since improvements are continuous,the net effect of this may be that an amount equal to $630,000 is recognised in profit or loss in the year ended 31 December 20X7. Even though continuous improvements and refining of the algorithms increases the value of the Al technology in excess of staff costs,it is not eligible for revaluation because it does not belong to an active market.The Al technology must therefore be measured at cost less accumulated amortisation and impairment losses. As well as the software and technology,Fuller holds raw data that it has collected and Al-powered information that it has processed.Both can be argued to meet the definition of an intangible asset;however,neither should be recognised as intangible assets because their cost cannot be reliably measured. Usefulness to investors representation of an entity's underlying performance and position.To be relevant,Financial information is useful to investors if it is relevant and offers a faithful representation of an entity's underlying performance and position.To be relevant, information must be capable of making a difference in their decision to hold or sell information must be capable of making a difference in their decision to hold or sell shares,.Investors in Fuller are therefore interested in information that will help them to assess future potential returns. Together,the raw data,data management system,Al technology including continually improved algorithms and Al-powered information will significantly increase the value of Fuller and enhance its ability to generate cash flows. Therefore,information about these aspects of the business are relevant to investors through their predictive value.The fact that the raw data and Al-powered information are not recognised at all and the Al technology is measured at cost limits the relevance of Fuller's financial statements to its investors. For financial statements to faithfully represent what they purport to represent,they should be complete,neutral and free from error.It may also be argued that without full information about intangible assets,the information provided in Fuller's financial statements is not complete and so the objective of faithful representation is not met. A counter-argument would suggest that recognition of Fuller's raw data and Al- generated information as an intangible asset would not result in faithful representation because any value attributed to it would be too subjective to be neutral. understandable and comparable.Investors are likely to understand the value ofThe usefulness of financial information to investors is enhanced if it is both understandable and comparable.Investors are likely to understand the value of information to a business,meaning that lack of understandability is not a reason to information to a business,meaning that lack of understandability is not a reason to exclude it from the statement of financial position.It may also be argued that the inconsistency in the current accounting treatment of intangible assets,whereby some are recognised and others are not,creates confusion and leads to a lack of understanding of financial information. In order to compare technology-based businesses that hold significant levels of data on their customers and markets,investors must have access to information about this data.Recognition of information-based intangible assets would allow for better comparison,and so make financial statements more useful,however it must be included on a consistent basis to allow for comparability.Currently this is not addressed in IFRS Accounting Standards. At present,Fuller's investors must rely on management commentary and other narrative reports that accompany the financial statements to gain information about data and Al-powered information.This information is less accessible to investors, less comparable and therefore less useful than information included in the financial statements.Fuller's investors would benefit from the recognition of information- related intangible assets in its statement of financial position,however this would require the development of new accounting guidance or IFRS Accounting Standards. Detailed Marking Guide-Fuller Co (a) (i) Discussion and application of the following to the scenario: Performance obligations Agents and principals 4 8 5 5Revenue recognition 4 8 5 5 (ii) 1 mark per discussion point (b) Discussion and application of the following to the scenario: Purchased intangible assets Subsequent expenditure Amortisation Revaluation Internally generated assets Discussion of usefulness Professional skills marks Total Marks 4 3 max max 1 3 1 1 1 6 10 max 2 25 Suggested Solution-Enriquez Co (a)Consideration IFRS 3 Business Combinations says that consideration transferred to acquire control over a subsidiary should be measured at fair value. The deferred consideration should be recognised at its fair value of $31.7 million ($35mx 1/1.052).This will increase goodwill,and a liability should be recognised for the same amount. During the financial year,the discount on the liability should be unwound.An interest expense of $1.6 million ($31.7m×5%)should be charged to the consolidated statement of profit or loss,and the carrying amount of the liability increased to $33.3 million($31.7m+$1.6m).As at the reporting date,the liability should be presented as a current liability on the consolidated statement of financial position. Net assets IFRS 3 states that the identifiable net assets of a subsidiary should be measured and recognised at acquisition at fair value. The subsidiary's property,plant and equipment at acquisition needed to be measured at its fair value of $40 million.This is an increase of $30 million($40m- $10m),which will reduce goodwill by the same amount. This $30 million fair value uplift needs to be depreciated over the building's remaining 15-year useful life.This amounts to $2 million ($30m/15)in the current financial year,which is charged to the consolidated statement of profit or loss.Of this expense,$1.2 million($2m x60%)is attributable to the owners of Enriquez Co and the remaining $0.8 million is attributable to the non-controlling interest (NCl). Deferred tax The fair value uplift of $30 million creates a taxable temporary difference in the consolidated financial statements.A deferred tax liability of $6 million($30m x20%) should be recognised at acquisition,which will increase goodwill by the same amount. By the reporting date,the taxable temporary difference has reduced to $28 million ($30m -$2m depreciation).The deferred tax liability should be adjusted to $5.6 million($28m x20%).As such,the deferred tax liability should be reduced by $0.4 million,with a corresponding credit posted against the tax expense in the consolidated statement of profit or loss.Of this credit $0.24 million($0.4m x60%)is attributable to the owners of Enriquez Co and $0.16 million($0.4m×40%)is attributable to the NCI. NCI The fair value of the identifiable net assets at acquisition was $104 million ($80m + $30m-$6m).The NCl at acquisition should therefore be recognised at $41.6 million ($104mx40%).This means that the NCl at acquisition must be increased by $9.6 million($41.6m-$32m),with a corresponding increase to goodwill. Goodwill A revised goodwill calculation is as follows: $m Cash consideration 50.0 Deferred consideration($35m x1/1.052) 31.7 NCl at acquisition (40%x$104m) 41.6 Carrying amount of net assets at acquisition 80.0 Fair value adjustment($40m-$10m) 30.0 Deferred tax liability($30m x20%) (6.0) ----- (104.0) ------ Goodwill at acquisition 19.3 ----- (b) Share sale Enriquez Co stillhas control over Salz Co after the sale of shares,meaning that Salz Co remains a subsidiary. Goodwill should not be recalculated,and no gain or loss arises on the transaction in the consolidated financial statements.The $22 million recognised as other income in the consolidated statement of profit or loss must therefore be removed. The transaction must be accounted for in equity,as an increase to the NCI. At the date of the share sale,the net assets of Salz Co were carried in the consolidated financial statements at $158 million($146m+($18m-$6m)).The increase in the NCl is therefore $15.8 million ($158m x10%). The difference between the proceeds received and the increase in the NCl is $6.2 million($22m-$15.8m).This is recognised as an increase in other components of equity. His professional behaviour should avoid any action that discredits the profession.He should seek to protect and build the company's reputation by not engaging in conduct that might undermine its respect and market trust. It is inevitable that the substandard seals on the medicines would come to light,and the company would potentially be charged with malpractice and face possible criminal charges. Ethical accountants must abide by laws,rules and regulations relating to their business activities and Mr Chan will be breaking the law by failing to recall the medicine.If litigation was brought against the company,it would likely cost the company more than $1 million needed to recall the medicine,and there would be brand and reputational damage.Mr Chan is not being objective by ignoring the longer-term costs,regulatory requirements,reputational loss and the public interest. This is the danger of short-term decision making without applying an ethical framework.There is a significant issue where accountants concentrate purely on profitability,without thinking of the ethical implications.Mr Chan's objectives seem to be to appease shareholders,increase profits,reduce costs and maximise his bonus. Mr Chan's suggestion to the Board that they could try to buy back the medicines via third parties in order to cover up the issue shows a lack of honesty and truthfulness in all his dealings and a desire to deliberately mislead and deceive others. The cost cutting programme could affect several different products being developed. There is no humanitarian incentive in his cost cutting or in his pricing policy of overcharging for the medicines.Mr Chan's business practices may affect the public good by preventing individuals from receiving their medication if the medicines are too expensive.His policy is essentially to manipulate market prices by acquiring companies currently holding medicine patents thus making a profit without investing in new research and development. Mr Chan should act fairly and justly in all his dealings and not exercise power arbitrarily.Accountants should ensure that the decisions and recommendations that they make are of the highest standard.Mr Chan has a role in furthering the legitimate aims of Refer Co,and an ongoing duty to avoid any actions that may bring the profession into disrepute.He should reinforce the values of the organisation and support its commitment to high standards leading to increased public confidence in the profession.He should seek business objectives that cause the least harm and the greatest positive good.He should demonstrate respect for the rights and interests of all those who have a stake in his decisions,treating people with equal respect and dignity. (b)An entity should account for a contract with a customer in accordance with IFRS 15 Revenue from Contracts with Customers when: · the contract has been approved by the parties to the contract; ·each party's rights in relation to the goods or services to be transferred can be identified; ● the payment terms for the goods or services to be transferred can be identified; ● the contract has commercial substance;and ● it is probable that the consideration to which the entity is entitled to in exchange for the goods or services will be collected. The most relevant criterion in this situation is that the entity should account for the contract when it is probable that the entity willbe able to collect the consideration to which it is entitled.In evaluating collectability,Refer Co should consider the client's ability and intention to pay.Refer Co must decide whether it is appropriate to recognise new sales to this government,as revenue should be recognised only when it is probable that the entity will collect the consideration. Slow payment does not,on its own,preclude revenue recognition.However,it might affect the amount of revenue that can be recognised.This is because the transaction price,and the receivable,willneed to be discounted at initial recognition if payment is not going to be received for several years. Also,Refer Co is attempting to negotiate a repayment plan which may include a price concession.In this case,the consideration becomes variable and Refer Co will need to estimate the variable consideration and the amount that it expects to receive.If the directors of Refer Co believe that it will receive less than the invoiced amount,then it will have to evaluate on 31 December 20X7 whether this is an implicit price concession or whether the receivable is impaired. Receivables are a financial asset.In accordance with IFRS 9 Financial Instruments, a loss allowance must be recognised.IFRS 9 permits a simplified approach for trade receivables,which is that the loss allowance should always be equal to lifetime expected credit losses. When measuring the lifetime expected credit loss,the amount of consideration included in the impairment test is based on an estimate of the amounts that the entity expects to receive.This will include an estimate of variable consideration and the effect of the customer's credit risk.If the receivables are overdue,they are likely to be credit impaired.In this case,lifetime expected credit losses should be calculated as the difference between the gross carrying amount of the receivables and the present value of the expected future cash flows. Marking Guide -Refer (a) (i) Discussion and application of key ethical principles relating to sub- standard medicine seals(ii) Discussion and application of key ethical principles relating to cost cutting and acquisition of companies standard medicine seals (b) Discussion and application of the following principles: -IFRS 15 -collectability -price concessions -discounting and impairment Professional marks Total Marks Available 5 5 Marks Maximum 10 2 2 2 3 2 2 2 3 8 2 20 Suggested Solution-Jarid Co (a)Server contract contains a lease The lease accounting guidance in IFRS 16 Leases applies to any arrangement that conveys control over an identified asset to another party.A customer controls the use of the identified asset by possessing the right to obtain substantially all of the economic benefits from the asset and the right to direct the use of the identified asset throughout the period of the arrangement. Jarid Co has the right to control the use of the server because it has the right to obtain all of the economic benefits from the use of the server based on its exclusive access. Servicio Co cannot determine how the server is used during the term of the arrangement,nor can it decide whether to reconfigure the server or use it for another purpose. Jarid Co also has the right to direct the use of the server because of its ability to determine which data which is stored on the server. Jarid Co has also integrated the server into its operations.Maintenance and support activities ensure that the server operates as it should,but do not affect the use of the server nor the economic benefits derived by Jarid Co. The contract explicitly identifies that the server can only be changed in the event of malfunction.Therefore,the arrangement contains identified equipment.An identified asset can be specified explicitly or implicitly.However,if the supplier has a contractual right to substitute the asset,then,the asset is not identified.This is not the case with Jarid Co's arrangement as,in addition to the above,Servicio Co cannot determine how the server is used during the term of the arrangement,nor can it decide whether to reconfigure the server or use it for another purpose. An identified asset must be physically distinct.A physically distinct asset may be an entire asset or a portion of an asset.In this case,the server is explicitly identified in the contract and so it meets the identification criteria. Measurement of right-of-use asset and lease liability The lease liability is measured by considering the fixed payments due less any lease incentive receivable.Jarid Co should measure the initial lease liability at 1 April 20X2 by calculating the present value of the four unpaid annual fixed lease payments of $1.05 million discounted at the implicit interest rate of 5%($1.05m x3.546),which is $3.72 million.The cash back of $525,000 is discounted to its present value on 1 April 20X2 of $500,000($525,000/1.05).As such the lease liability is initially measured at $3.22 million ($3.72m+$500,000). The right-of-use asset would initially be equal to the lease liability,plus the initial annual payment of $1.05 million paid on the lease commencement date and the legal costs incurred of $0.1 million.Thus,the right-of-use asset will be $4.37 million ($3.22m+$1.05m+$0.1m). During the year to 31 March 20X3,interest would be charged on the outstanding lease liability.Depreciation on the right-of-use asset would be charged over the lease term. w1 Lease liability as at 31 March 20X3 Date 31 March 20X3 b/d $m 3.22 cash flow $m 0 effectiveinterest (5%) effective $m 0.16 c/d $m 3.38 w2 ROU asset as at 31 March 20X3 Initial cost 1 April 20X2: lease liability Initial lease payment paid $m 3.22 1.05 Legal fees paid Depreciation to 31 March 20X3($4.37m/5 years) ROU asset carrying amount at 31 March 20X3 Thus,the right-of-use asset at 31 March 20X3 would be $3.5 million ($4.37m- $0.87m). The lease liability would be $3.38 million. Profit or loss willbe charged with interest on the lease liability of $0.16 million and depreciation of $0.87 million. Tutorial Note:The accounting entries would be as follows: Right of use asset Lease liability Cash (rent paid) Dr(m) 4.27 Cr(m) 3.22 1.05 Accounting for right-of-use asset,lease liability and advance rental Right-of-use asset 0.1 Cash (legal costs paid) Accounting for the legal costs incurred on contract 0.1 Finance cost ($3.22m x5%) 0.16 Lease liability 0.16 Accounting for the interest charged on the outstanding lease liability to 31 March 20X3 Expense -profit or loss($4.37m/5) 0.874 Right-of-use asset Accounting for the depreciation on the right-of-use asset 0.874 (b)(i) Cash and cash equivalents IAS 7 Statement of Cash Flows defines cash as comprising cash on hand and demand deposits.IAS 32 Financial Instruments:Presentation states that currency is a financial asset because it represents a medium of exchange and currency is generally accounted for as cash.Cryptocurrency by definition suggests that it is cash but generally,it seems that cryptocurrencies represent a limited medium of exchange as they are not supported by a central bank or recognised as legal tender in most jurisdictions. In addition,cryptocurrencies do not seem to meet the definition of a cash equivalent in IAS 7 which is "short-term,highly liquid investments that are readily convertible to known amounts of cash and which are subject to an insignificant risk of changes in value”.They do not have a short-term life,they have significant short-term value changes and are subject to significant risk.Thus,at present,cryptocurrencies do not qualify to be accounted for as cash or a cash equivalent. Financial assets As regards cryptocurrencies meeting the definition of a financial asset,they are not an investment in the equity of another entity.For financial assets that are debt instruments,there must be a contractual right to receive cash or another financial asset from another entity or to exchange financial assets or financial liabilities with another entity.The holder of a cryptocurrency generally does not have any such contractual right.Therefore,cryptocurrencies do not seem to meet the definition of a non-cash financial asset in accordance with IAS 32 and IFRS 9 Financial Instruments.However,certain contracts to buy or sell cryptocurrencies in the future may meet the definition of a derivative and be subject to IFRS 9. Intangible assets An intangible asset is defined in IAS 38 Intangible Assets as an identifiable non- monetary asset without physical substance.Cryptocurrencies generally do not meet the definition of monetary assets in IAS 38 which refers to assets to be received in fixed or determinable amounts of money.Hence,it appears that cryptocurrencies,at present,are best accounted for under IAS 38,which means initial recognition at cost and subsequent measurement at either cost or at fair value. (b)(ii) Measuring fair value IAS 38 only allows the revaluation approach when active markets exist for an intangible asset.Therefore,in order to recognise and value the cryptocurrency,Jarid Co will have to utilise IFRS 13 Fair Value Measurement.IFRS 13 sets out a framework for determining fair values.IFRS 13 uses an exit price methodology where fair value is determined on the assumptions that market participants would use when pricing an asset.Fair values are divided into a three-level fair value hierarchy: ·Level 1:quoted prices in active markets for identical assets or liabilities the entity can access at the measurement date ·Level 2:observable inputs other than Level 1 inputs ·Level 3:unobservable inputs. IFRS 13 gives precedence to observable inputs over unobservable inputs. In determining the fair value of a cryptocurrency,the first step would be to see whether an active market exists for that currency.An active market is one in which transactions take place with sufficient frequency and volume to provide pricing information on an ongoing basis.In this case,there are several markets for the cryptocurrency that may meet the definition of an active market and each market has different prices.In this situation,IFRS 13 requires that the principal market is determined.This is the market with the greatest volume and level of activity which Jarid Co can access,as not all markets are freely accessible to the entity. There are several active markets,but some have restrictions.For example,market A only allows trading for values less than 10,000 dollars which in Jarid Co's case does not cover the quantity of cryptocurrency that Jarid Co holds.In market B,trading in cryptocurrency needs a licence which can only be obtained by financial institutions and in market E,although there is no direct ban on the trading of the cryptocurrency, there are very strict rules which makes trading in cryptocurrency a difficult process. There are two markets with approximately the same level of activity,markets C and D.In this case,where there is no clear principal market,the next step under IFRS 13 is to default to the most advantageous market within the group of active markets with the highest activity levels to which the entity has access.The most advantageous market is the one that maximises the amount that would be received to sell the asset,after taking into account transaction costs and transport costs.The most advantageous market is therefore D.The final step is to measure the fair value of the cryptocurrency using the closing price in Market D,which is $6,350 per unit. Impact on financial statements The cryptocurrency would be valued at $6,350 per unit,creating a carrying amount of $6,350,000($6,350×1000 units)in intangible assets at the year end of 31 March 20X3.Under IAS 38,the cryptocurrency would have initially been recorded at its cost of $6,500,000($6,500×1000 units)and therefore a revaluation loss of $150,000 should be recognised in profit or loss. Tutorial note: The asset's fair value hierarchy category has been assessed as Level 1;valuation is at the market price for that unit of account times the number of units held. The cryptocurrency market is volatile such that,the time at which Jarid Co values the cryptocurrency may be significant and thus consistency of application of that policy is required.Not all cryptocurrencies will meet the criteria for Level 1 in the fair value hierarchy. Total 9 3 2 2 7 3 6 9 25 Suggested solution -Anchali (a)(i) Restructure IAS 37 Provisions,Contingent Liabilities and Contingent Assets defines a restructuring as a program that is planned and controlled by management and that materially changes the scope of a business or the way it is conducted.It includes sale or termination of a line of business,closure of business locations,changes in management structure and fundamental reorganisations. In the case of Anchali Co,the restructuring is material and fundamental as the business is being reorganised into three companies.The changes to Anchali Co would therefore meet the 'fundamental reorganisation'criteria as they will materially affect the focus of the company's operations. (a)(ii) Constructive obligation Restructuring costs are recognised when there is a present obligation(legal or constructive)resulting from a past event,and a reliable estimate of costs can be made. A constructive obligation is an obligation where the entity's past practice,published policies,or current statements create an expectation in other parties that it will accept and discharge certain responsibilities. A constructive obligation to restructure arises when there is a formal plan that sets out: ·the parts of the business and principal locations that are affected; ·the location,function and approximate number of employees whose services will be terminated; ● the expenditure that will be undertaken;and ·when the plan will be implemented and completed. Moreover,to create a constructive obligation,there must be a valid expectation in those affected that the entity would carry out the restructuring. Normally,a board decision (20 October 20X8)does not in itself establish a constructive obligation.This is because a valid expectation that the obligation will be discharged cannot be created until those affected are made aware of the plan. The formal plan was discussed at a meeting with affected employees on 1 November 20X8.Assuming this was carried out in sufficient detail,a constructive obligation to restructure would have been created. On 3 November 20X8,the board announced in a press release that some restaurants will close and this announcement attracted significant publicity in the media.At this stage,the company cannot withdraw the commitment and therefore, practically,it cannot avoid the obligation.However,as noted,a constructive obligation had already been created on 1 November 20X8. The constructive obligation gets translated into contractual obligations when the enterprise enters into binding contracts in the process of implementing the board's decision.This occurs on 10th December,when company enters into binding contracts to purchase new head offices for the hotel and leisure companies. (a)(ii) Measurement and recognition In general,the costs of a restructuring are recognised when it meets the definition of a restructuring as per IAS 37 and management has an obligation to proceed with it. Provisions are measured at the best estimate of the expenditure required to settle the obligation.Only incremental costs that are directly associated with the restructuring should be included in the provision.Costs associated with ongoing activities,such as the cost of training or relocating continuing staff,cannot be included in Anchali Co's restructuring provision.The expenditure included in the provision should be discounted using a pre-tax rate that reflects both the time value of money and risks specific to the liability.Given the restructuring is anticipated to be completed by 31 March 20X9,this may not be material. Provisions are never recognised for future operating losses,even in a restructuring. Goodwill Under IFRS 3 Business Combinations,a provision for restructuring would be recognised as a liability of Stencil Co at the acquisition date if an obligation existed at this date.If so,it would increase goodwill arising on the business combination.In this case,the constructive obligation was established on 1 November 20X8,but the acquisition of Stencil Co occurred on 20 October 20X8.Therefore,the costs will not be recognised as a liability of Stencil Co acquisition.This means that the restructuring will not impact on goodwill but will instead be expensed to profit or loss. (a)(iv)I nvestor communication When communicating with investors about the restructuring,it is important to be entity-specific and set out simply and directly the company's plans.There is no point disclosing information that is readily available outside the financial statements,for example from the press release.The communication should be organised in a way that highlights important matters.For example,the closure of some restaurants and the impact that this may have on revenue and profitability.The fact that revenue will be challenged and unpredictable for the foreseeable future is important to investors. Communicating this new,uncertain outlook will be challenging for Anchali Co. Initially,Anchali Co should disclose as much information as possible to investors.By law,Anchali Co must provide copies of the restructuring-related documents and incorporate investors comments in them. Anchali Co should have an ongoing dialogue with investors.For example,investors willbe pleased that there has been an agreement with employee representatives, and this should be communicated to them.If there is a need to revise the plans,then investors should be informed at the earliest opportunity.Periodic updates should be given to investors to keep them informed through earnings calls,press releases,and teleconferencing.Investors are reliant on the financial information provided by companies. Investors should be informed that the company is dealing with unprecedented levels of uncertainty such that the finance team is struggling with producing a reliable forecast of business performance.The projected costs and their financial impact should be included in the communication.The company must be confident in any revised financial data that is issued as a second revision tends to provoke a much more negative reaction than the first. Anchali Co should emphasise that in the long term the restructure willadd value to the business and that restructuring is a way for companies to optimise the performance of their business.Companies often restructure during a downturn,so that they are in a good position to take advantage of any future improvement in business conditions.The company should set out the nature of the rationalisation of their product offering as the company is switching to three main business areas, which should improve efficiency.In the long term,investors should be told that operating costs willbe cut significantly again with the aim of improving operating efficiency across the business. Investors will only invest in a restructuring opportunity if they have confidence that the management can see the restructuring through.The fact that the restructuring willbe completed by 31 March 20X9 should give investors'confidence.The starting point for any investment is the valuation of the company.If the prospect of restructuring,and improved returns,is already captured by the share price then there would be no reason for investors to invest. (b) Benefits Employee termination benefits are provided in exchange for the termination of an individual's employment,outside of normal retirement.Employee termination benefits are in the scope of IAS 19 Employee Benefits rather than IAS 37.The costs are recognised at the earlier of when the company can no longer withdraw the offer of those benefits and when the company recognises costs for restructuring under IAS 37.Assessing the point at which an offer cannot be withdrawn may be complex and depends on whether the employee's acceptance is required and whether the termination is voluntary rather than involuntary.In this case the offer is involuntary but there is a degree of further service required. Anchali Co should therefore assess the terms and conditions of the termination benefits arrangement to ensure that the benefits are either termination or short-term employee benefits.Benefits conditional on future services are considered short-term employee benefits and are accrued over an employee's service period rather than at a single point in time. The amount that Anchali Co would have to pay for terminating employment regardless of whether the employees stay or leave before closure is $5,000 per employee.The termination of the employees'employment is a result of the entity's decision to close the restaurants.Therefore,Anchali Co should recognise a liability of $1.75 million (350×$5,000)for the termination benefits. According to IAS 19,a termination benefits offer cannot be withdrawn once the plan establishes the termination benefits in sufficient detail for employees to determine the type and amount of benefits they will receive.This happens on 3 November, when the benefits payable was agreed with employee representatives.However,the termination benefits can be provided for on 1 November because this is the earlier of when the company can no longer withdraw the offer of those benefits and when the company recognises restructuring costs. The extra benefits that employees willreceive if they remain until 31 March 20X9 are in exchange for services provided over that period and are short-term employee benefits.This is because Anchali Co expects to pay them before twelve months after 31 December 20X8.Thus,an expense of $180,000((150×($11,000-$5000))/5)is recognised each month until 31 March 20X9($900,000 in total)with a corresponding increase in the carrying amount of the liability.
